section_name: The Effects of Privatization on Firm Productivity in China
text: Abstract. We study the productivity effects of the world’s largest privatization program in China. Relying on a matching and difference-in-difference procedure, we find that privatizations robustly led to large productivity gains. Privatizations improved TFP by 13 to 15 percent, and this estimate is at the high-end of the estimates obtained from other transitional countries. Further evidence suggests that productivity gains came from improvement in management quality. The positive effect of privatization on TFP was more pronounced among firms in more competitive industries, suggesting complementarity between competition and privatizations. Privatizations worked less well for “commanding-heights” SOEs under the oversight of the central government. Our evidence suggests that the privatization program in China was a key factor behind China’s growth in the past decades.

section_name: Introduction
text: After the Thatcher government popularized large-scale privatizations four decades ago, privatizations have been widely used throughout the world, especially by developing and transition countries with large shares of state-owned enterprises (SOEs). The literature on the effects of privatization is huge, as shown in several influential surveys across different generations of research (Megginson and Netter 2001; Djankov and Murrell 2002; Estrin et al. 2009; Megginson 2017). These surveys suggest modest and diverse gains in firm performance, while acknowledging the severe selectivity of privatization.

section_name: Introduction
text: Most studies on privatization focus on CEE (Central and Eastern Europe) and CIS countries (i.e., Commonwealth of Independent States, countries formerly affiliated with the Soviet Union), with occasional exceptions on other large countries such as the United States, Mexico, and India (Megginson and Netter 2001; Djankov and Murrell 2002; Estrin et al. 2009). They show that the effects of privatization differ by the methods and the institutional context of privatizations (Djankov and Murrell 2002; Estrin et al. 2009): more positive effects or associations, for instance, have been consistently found in CEE than in CIS countries. Much less work is done on privatizations in China. Estrin et al. (2009) concludes, “There are as yet no TFP studies using data from China that employ robust methodologies and, perhaps because of this, the available papers find diverse results, with the effect of nonstate ownership being mostly positive but sometimes statistically insignificant and sometimes negative” (Estrin et al. 2009, p. 702).

section_name: Introduction
text: This deficiency has not been successfully addressed in the past decade, even though this period has witnessed strong interest in, and more studies on, privatizations in China. In a recent survey on privatization around the world, Megginson (2017) finds that in the new literature on privatizations, papers on China accounted for the largest share among all regions. However, most are on share issue privatizations, that is, partial privatization for publicly listed SOEs with the government retaining strong, and most likely, dominant control, and these account for a tiny share of privatizations in China. Under the slogan of “grabbing the big and letting go the small,” the overwhelming majority of privatized SOEs under the SOE restructuring program near the turn of this century were much smaller and non-listed, and they were afterwards truly private firms (Xu et al. 2005; Hsieh and Song 2015). Since the majority of privatizations in China were full privatizations whose effects are likely more pronounced than partial privatization (Li and Xu 2004; Megginson 2005), and share issue privatizations accounted for only around one percent of all privatizations in China, convincing assessment of the privatization effects in China remains rare, yet understanding the effects of China’s privatization is important in light of the diverse interpretation of the Chinese experience. Unbeknown to most, China’s privatization scheme at the turn of the century is the largest privatization program in history (Megginson, Nash and Van Randenborgh 1994). The total amount of worldwide revenues raised by privatization, as estimated by the end of the last century, is [FORMULA] 700 billion (Gan, Guo and Xu 2017). Despite the gigantic privatization program, the Chinese government’s involvement in the economy has also been among the heaviest in the world (Shirley and Xu, forthcoming), and China’s experience has been a key reason behind the popularity of state capitalism. Based on China Statistical Yearbooks, as of 2018, SOEs still accounted for 27 percent of total industrial output, and 13 percent of total urban employment in China. Is China’s growth in the past decades partly attributable to privatizations and other marketization reforms, or to its state involvement, industrial policies and a large SOE sector?

section_name: Introduction
text: Not surprisingly, both interpretations of China’s growth record have strong proponents. Some pundits emphasize strong government involvement as being fundamental to Chinese growth. Ramo (2004), for instance, coined the term Beijing Consensus, to characterize China’s unique approach to managing its economy. Others quickly embraced his interpretation and endorsed strong government involvement in the economy (Halper 2010). Some economists have also embraced the strong government interpretation, including industrial policies (Lin 2012; Rodrik 2006; Stiglitz 1994, 2008), or the sufficiency of competition without changes in state ownership (Lin, Cai, and Li 1998). In contrast, many other economists argue that China’s growth in the past decades reflects its adoption of common recommendations such as marketization and privatization (Zhu 2012; Brandt, Kambourov, and Storesletten 2016; Megginson 2017; Wu 2018; Zhang 2019; Shirley and Xu, forthcoming). Given the importance of sound policy recommendations based on the Chinese experience, understanding the real effects of privatization in China is thus critically important for offering policy advice on achieving growth in developing countries.

section_name: Introduction
text: Using a comprehensive data set that covers all Chinese industrial SOEs from 1998 to 2007, we study the impact of privatization on firm productivity. First, without dealing with time-varying selectivity, we use ordinary least squares and fixed-effects specifications. Second, although the central theme of the paper is not causality, we try to mitigate the concerns of selection by using a matching and difference-in-difference (matching-DID) procedure to compare privatized firms with otherwise similar firms based on observable characteristics. In particular, for each treatment firm that experienced privatization during the sample period, we find a control SOE with similar observables (being in the same four-digit industry in the same year, and in terms of the propensity score) that did not experience privatization, and compare their respective before-after difference in productivity. Here, since the control firm did not experience privatization, we assign its observation year in terms of the before and the after status to be the same as that of its corresponding treatment firm. We further control for the treatment-control pair fixed effects to filter out common shocks, as well as other city-level macro indicators and industry-year fixed effects. Results based on the matched procedure show that privatizations lead to increases in firms’ productivity by about 13 to 15 percent. We find that privatizations in China are associated with productivity improvements within the range of effects found in the literature on Eastern European and CIS countries, and the magnitudes are on the high end of that broad range. We also find that the main source of the large productivity gains from privatizations was improvement in management efforts and quality. The positive effect of privatization on TFP is much more pronounced among firms in more competitive industries—specifically, for SOEs in industries that are at the 25% percentile of industry-level competition (as measured by the Herfindahl index), the privatization effect on TFP was only less than 30 percent of that of SOEs in industries that are at the 75% of industry-level competition. Competition and privatization are thus complementary. We also find that privatizations did not improve productivity at all for SOEs under the oversight of the central government, which the government wanted to control for strategic purposes.

section_name: Introduction
text: Our paper adds to the literature on the effects of privatizations. Most previous studies find that privatizations are associated with better firm performance, while other studies show that government ownership is not necessarily less efficient than private ownership. We contribute by adding evidence from China. China has seen more privatizations than anywhere else in the world (Megginson 2017), yet we still lack credible evidence on the effects of privatization on productivity. Our research extends earlier work on how China’s privatizations have affected firm performance. Existing work has mostly focus on share issue privatization (SIP) of listed firms (Sun and Tong 2003; Jiang, Yue, and Zhao 2009; Deng, Gan, and He 2010), which only accounted for one percent of privatization. SIPs received the most research in China primarily due to the better availability of data (Megginson, 2017). Covering the more prevalent and typical types of privatizations like us, Bai, Lu and Tao (2009) use the early version of the ASIF dataset (covering1998-2005) to examine the privatization effects of non-listed firm. Using fixed effects model, they find that privatizations increase firm sales but do not increase profitability. Compared with Bai, Lu and Tao (2009), we use the ASIF set covering a longer period and employ matching procedures to mitigate selection bias. Also, we focus on examining the impacts of privatization on firm TFP, a thorough measure of productivity. We find that eliminating selection bias is vital for obtaining a plausible estimate of the privatization effects. Regressions based on the full sample imply that privatizations increase firm TFP by only 5%, while regressions on the matched sample show that privatizations increase firm TFP by 13-15%. We also offer evidence that privatization and competition are complementary in China, and that privatization does not improve productivity when non-economic incentives are strong (as in the case of “commanding-heights” SOEs). By offering plausible estimates of pronounced effects of privatization in China, we provide evidence that the privatization of SOEs was a key factor behind China’s economic takeoff.

section_name: Institutional Background
text: After China started marketization reforms in 1978, the traditionally peripheral parts of the economy such as the township-village enterprises and urban individual firms emerged and flourished (Chang and Wang, 1994), while SOEs, thanks to competition from TVEs as well as reforms such as delegation of control rights and stronger incentives, improved productivity in the 1980s (Li, 1997; Shirley and Xu, 2001). As competition continued to intensify from entry of private firms and foreign firms in the 1990s, the competitive positions of SOEs steadily deteriorated: the profits and taxes per unit of net capital stock and working capital in industrial SOEs kept on falling, dropping from 24 percent in 1978 to 7 percent in 1996 (Qian 2000), with more than one-third of SOEs having losses. Starting from the early-1990s, some local governments experimented with de facto privatizations (Garnaut, Song and Yao 2008). The rising losses of SOEs, the fiscal burdens, coupled with success of local privatization experiments, propelled the reform-oriented central government to sanction large-scale privatizations in the second half of the 1990s. In 1997, the central government announced its policy to restructure the state sector by allowing local governments to use various ways to restructure SOEs, including privatizations. By mid-2000s, the majority of SOEs had been privatized.

section_name: Institutional Background
text: Given the continental size of China that historically has always featured vast regional variations (Pomeranz, 2000), as well as the decentralized implementation of SOE restructuring and privatizations and distinct political incentives embedded in the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) promotion system, there were large variations in the method to privatize SOEs. Surprisingly, little has been written on the details of privatizations in China, and the little that have been written focus on share issue privatization, which only accounted for one percent of privatizations but received the most research in China due to the availability of data (Megginson, 2017).

section_name: Institutional Background
text: Overall, management and ownership changes after privatizations appeared to facilitate restructuring success. Most new owners of privatized firms were managers of the same SOEs ﻿before privatizations (Chen et al. 2020). Before the privatization program began, the CEOs that often became post-privatization owners had typically been selected to execute performance contracts. Such contracts often featured strong incentives such as performance bond that could be confiscated if performance targets were not fulfilled, and the CEOs were generally competent (Groves et al. 1994; Shirley and Xu 2001). This owner selection process thus facilitated SOE restructuring after privatizations. The ownership structure of privatized firms was concentrated, with the largest shareholder taking 60%, and the second and third largest shareholders adding up to 26% of shares (Gan, Guo and Xu 2017). In management buyouts (MBOs) and in ownership transfer to outsiders, the largest shareholders held 37% and 64% of total shares, respectively. When other methods were used, the largest shareholders held on average 91% of firm ownership. Non-dispersed ownership after privatizations has been found to be a key behind restructuring success (Djankov and Murrell 2002; Estrin et al. 2009).

section_name: Data
text: Our data set is the Annual Survey of Industrial Firms (ASIF) from 1998 to 2009, collected by China’s National Bureau of Statistics. It includes all SOEs and all non-state firms with sales above five million yuan in the industrial sectors, which include both manufacturing and mining; these firms account for about 90% of total industrial output value in China. In constructing our panel, we follow the best practices in fixing those identification inconsistencies that we knew.

section_name: Data
text: We construct a dummy variable, Privatized, that equals one for a firm for the privatization year and the subsequent years, and 0 otherwise. Our SOE definition here is the same as in Hsieh and Song (2015): a firm is state-owned when the state-owned share exceeds 50 percent, or when the state is reported as the controlling shareholder. An SOE is classified as being privatized when the state share drops below 50 percent, or the state is no longer reported as being the controlling shareholder. Out of 80,524 SOEs in our sample, at least 14,916 had been privatized by the end of our sample period. Figure 1 plots the year-by-year number of firms being privatized in our sample period. The city-level data also contains macroeconomic variables including the city-level GDP and fiscal revenue and expenditures, all from various issues of China City Statistical Yearbook. GDP and fiscal indicators are adjusted to the 1990 constant price with the province-specific GDP deflators from the China Statistical Yearbook. Tables 1 and 2 provide the definitions of the variables and their summary statistics. The average TFP of the sample is around 1.83, with a standard deviation of 1.92.

section_name: Effects of Privatization on Firm Productivity
text: In this section we first present estimates on the association of privatizations and productivity based on conventional specifications, with and without firm fixed effects. We then rely on the matching procedure to estimate the effects of privatization.

section_name: The Conventional Estimators
text: We first estimate two conventional specifications from the literature: the ordinary least square (OLS), and the firm fixed effect (FE) model:

section_name: The Conventional Estimators
text: Here is the performance measures of firm i in city c and industry j in year t. Firm performance is measured by the total factor productivity (TFP). We primarily use two methods to compute TFP, i.e., the OLS production function (TFP_OLS) and the Olley-Pakes approach (TFP_OP). The latter approach is proposed by Olley and Pakes (1996) and is commonly employed to deal with various biases associated with estimating TFP with the traditional OLS method in the regression of value-added on capital and labor (the standard log-linear Cobb-Douglas production function). We also check the robustness with alternative productivity proxies. The dummy variable, , is defined as in the previous section. We control for , the city-level macro variables (i.e., GDP Per capita, GDP Growth, Gov’t Expenditure Growth, Gov’t Revenue Growth, and Fixed Investment Growth). To further control for potential differences in pre-existing trends, we also include lagged oneto three-period TFP in the regressions. We control for industry-year fixed effects,, which captures technological and regulatory shocks. In the OLS specification, we do not, but in the firm FE specification, we do, control for the firm-level FE, , which captures all firm-specific time-invariant factors. As illustrated in Table 3, based on the FE specification, which is preferred over the OLS specification due to the importance of controlling for firm-specific heterogeneity, privatizations in China have a robust and positive association with productivity. Privatizations are associated with a higher TFP by around 5 percent (i.e., 5.6% for TFP_OP or 4.9% for TFP_IN).

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: The privatizations of SOEs may be strongly related to time-varying firm characteristics. Government’s decision to privatize an SOE and its performance may be jointly determined by omitted variables, for example, the government may pick a firm for privatization because of its larger potential for productivity improvement. Although it is extremely difficult to eliminate the endogeneity bias, in this section, we attempt to alleviate this problem by using the propensity score matching approach. It should be mentioned that propensity score matching cannot yield a causal identification, but it can mitigate the selection bias by adding some nonlinear terms of the control variables (such as industry, firm size, and the pre-trend) that would affect both the likelihood of privatizing an SOE and firm productivity.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: In particular, we estimate the productivity effects based on a matched sample of treated and control firms. Appendix C describes the detailed matching procedures and here we provide a summary. We limit our treated sample to SOEs that were privatized between 1999 and 2007. We exclude firms that were privatized in 1998 since we do not have their pre-treatment TFP. We restrict the nontreated sample to SOEs that had never been privatized between 1998 and 2007.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: To construct the PSM sample, we select controls from the nontreated sample by first matching potential controls with a treatment firm exactly on the 4-digit industry and the year: that is, for a non-treated firm to be a candidate control firm for a particular treated firm, it must be in the same four-digit industry and in the same year as the treated privatized firm. In order to control for a firm’s initial characteristics, we conduct the propensity score matching on several other variables, including the logarithm of the number of employees, the percentage of the state share, the leverage ratio, the return on sales, and lagged oneto three-period TFP. We impose the common support condition: A non-treated firm is included as a control for a particular treated firm if the ratio of the propensity score of the treated to that of the control firm is between 0.9 and 1.1.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Using the matched sample, we then combine matching with a difference-in-difference (DID) method, that is, we implement the DID procedure using the matched sample. As recommended by Heckman, Ichimura, and Todd (1997, 1998), the matching-DID approach compares the change in outcomes from the preto post-privatization periods, thereby eliminating unobserved, time-invariant firm-level differences.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: The dependent variable in our baseline specification, is defined as the change in average TFP from three years before to three years after privatization:

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Note that the t subscript indexes the specific year of privatization for the treatment firm. For control firms, we obtain in the same way. And t subscript indexes year of pseudo privatization, which is the year of privatization of the treated firm for which it is matched to. By constructing , we obtain a cross-sectional data of matched sample. We also conduct robustness checks by using the change in the average TFP from two years before the privatization to two years after the privatization.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: We can then derive the difference in between each treated firm and the corresponding control firm, and directly obtain the statistics of this double-difference value. We can, of course, also use regressions to implement the matched DID method, that is, regressing on the treatment dummy. If we don’t add control variables, the average of the direct double-difference value will be identical with the estimated coefficient from the regression. However, by estimating the matched DID regressions, we can add control variables, thereby further controlling for other time-varying factors that both affect productivity and the decision to privatize.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Specifically, the DID regression uses the change in a firm’s TFP (D_TFP) as the dependent variable, that is, we estimate the following regression on the cross-sectional data of matched sample:

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Here j (from 1 to J) represents a treated firm; i indexes firms from 1 to I, representing all firms (i.e., both the treatment and the control firms). When a firm is a treatment firm (i.e., observation “ii”), i=j. When i≠j, the ij-th observation means that firm i is a control firm for firm j. In addition, t indexes the specific year of privatization. is a dummy variable that equals 1 for treated firms (i.e., being privatized in the sample period) and 0 for control firms in year t. δ is the effect of privatization on TFP.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: We are able to control for the treatment-pair fixed effects (i.e., ). is the change in the firm’s TFP from the pre-treatment to the post-treatment period—for the control firm, the post-treatment years are placebo treatment years. is a set of other control variables including city-level macro controls (i.e., GDP Per capita, GDP growth, government expenditure growth, government revenue growth, and fixed-asset growth), as well as the industry-year fixed effects to further account for industry-specific trend of productivity changes. is a vector of year dummies, which can account for common trend of productivity for all firms in the sample. is an idiosyncratic error.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Following the typical protocol of the propensity score matching method, we estimate the equation by adding the kernel weight for controls. Specifically, the final weight of each control is assigned as their kernel weight divided by the sum of the kernel weights for all controls for a particular treated firm, and the treated firm is given a weight of one. By adding this weight, controls that are more similar to the treated firms are more highly weighted. We also conduct the regressions without adding weights in Appendix Table C3, and our results remain unchanged.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Before presenting the regression results based on the matched sample, Table 4 shows whether the covariates are balanced before and after using the matching sample. We present the calculations of percentage reduction in the normalized difference for each variable. Treated firms have statistically significant differences with non-treated firms prior to the propensity score matching, but the gap is statistically not distinguishable from zero after the matching. This suggests that the matching procedure almost completely eliminates the observable pre-treatment differences between the treated and the control firms. In Appendix Table C2, we report more details on the sample size and the means of the main variables for the initial sample and the matched sample in the baseline regression. We also report the normalized difference between the two samples.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Table 5 reports the regression results based on the matched sample. Panel A does not control for the treatment-control pair fixed effects; Panel B does. The results in the two panels are similar. The results show that privatizations improve TFP_OP by around 14.7 percentage points, and TFP_OLS by a similar magnitude of 12.8 percentage points.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Following the literature of impact evaluation programs (Heckman and Hotz, 1989), we then estimate the specification with the pre-trend and dynamic effects of privatization—by allowing the treated firms to have distinct effects depend on the privatization-year clock, that is, 4+, 3, 2, 1 years before, the year of privatization, and 1, 2, and 3+ years after the privatization. That is, we define a base year as the year prior to the privatization (i.e., t-1) and compute productivity differences for each year from three years before and three years after the privatization: We estimate separate regressions similar to equation (3), to obtain each pre-privatization or post-privatization coefficient, that is, we conduct separate regressions on each s of . The pre-privatization coefficients can help us detect whether there is differential pre-trend between the treatment and the control groups. The post-privatization coefficients permit us to estimate the dynamic impacts of privatization in various years.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: Instead of reporting the regression table, we graph the results in Figure 2. Each dot represents the estimated pre-privatization or post-privatization coefficient, which is obtained from separate regressions on each s of . Prior to the privatization year, differences in TFP growth are tiny and not statistically distinguishable from zero, suggesting parallel trends between the treatment and the control firms. Immediately after the privatization, however, the treated firms have higher relative TFP growth than that of the control firms.

section_name: The estimation based on the matched sample
text: We can compare our results with the previous studies on Eastern European and CIS countries. Brown, Earle, and Telegdy (2006) find that privatizations led to productivity effects of 15 percent in Romania, 8 percent in Hungary, and 2 percent in Ukraine, but -3 percent in Russia. Brown, Earle, and Telegdy (2016) also report that privatizations raised firm labor productivity by approximately 10% in Romania, Hungary and Lithuania, but had zero or even negative effects in Ukraine and Russia. Our estimates of the effects of privatizations on productivity in China is close to the high end of this range, that is, close to that in Romania, but much larger than those in Hungary, Ukraine and Russia. Such a wide range of estimates on productivity effects of privatizations across countries suggest that the underlying macroeconomic and institutional environment matters a great deal (Megginson, 2017). The privatization literature has emphasized repeatedly and convincingly that the effects of privatization depend critically on the underlying economic and institutional backgrounds. When China undertook the privatization program in the late 1990s, SOEs were on average losing money, and their performance was at the lowest level possible. Political opposition to privatization was low, and local governments could experiment with various ways to privatize SOEs. Moreover, the liberalization reforms in the 1980s set up a solid platform for privatized firms to prosper (Li, Li and Zhang 2000; Huang 2012). Stronger market reforms in dimensions other than privatization, such as price liberalization, international trade and foreign exchange system, competition policy, financial sector reform, also tend to raise privatization effectiveness. Both before and during the privatization reforms, China had been growing fast (i.e., around 9 percent a year), while most transition countries in Eastern Europe and CIS countries had been stagnant. For instance, the annual GDP growth rate Russia and Ukraine was -0.03% and -2.1% on average between 1992 and 2005, but Hungary and Romania achieved a higher GDP growth rate at 2.7% and 2.2%, respectively. Brown, Earle, and Telegdy (2016) also show that national GDP growth is likely to raise the effect of privatization. Li and Xu (2004) present country-sectoral level evidence that the privatization effects are larger where competition is fiercer. The large effects of the Chinese SOE privatization program thus have their favorable pre-conditions and are likely to hold only in countries with a large loss-making SOE sector and favorable pre-privatization conditions such as strong competition and solid economic growth.

section_name: Sources of productivity improvement after privatization
text: To have a better understanding of the sources of the pronounced productivity effects of privatizations, similar to Brown, Earl and Telegdy (2006), we proceed to examine how privatizations affect the logarithm of value added, the logarithm of the number of employees, the logarithm of the capital stock, and the logarithm of total employee wages. We also examine access to finance as proxied by leverage ratio, as well as innovation (i.e., the ratio of new product sales to total sales). In all the regressions, we rely on the PSM approach similar to those in Table 5. The results are contained in Table 6.

section_name: Sources of productivity improvement after privatization
text: We find that privatizations led to firm expansion and employment restructuring, somewhat less use of external finance, and no changes in product innovations. After privatization, privatized firms, on average, increased their value added by 4.4%, while decreased their capital input by 5.2%, respectively. The decrease of capital after privatization is consistent with the reduced access to external finance, as measured by a decrease in the leverage ratio. We also find that privatization led to lower labor usage by 4.2%, suggesting that privatized firms reduced redundant employees after privatization. We do not find evidence that privatizations led to higher share of new products in the product portfolio (the estimated coefficient is even negative). These results suggest that the main sources of the large productivity gains from privatizations were not innovations, but improvement in management efforts and quality. Indeed, when politicians run SOEs, they have non-economic objectives such as maintaining excess employment to maintain political stability (Vickers and Yarrow, 1998). Moreover, since SOEs are not residual claimant, they naturally have less incentives to reduce costs and improve performance. Once privatized, the firm owner/manager is expected to benefit more from cost saving, and would accordingly work harder and/or improve management quality more.

section_name: Sources of productivity improvement after privatization
text: Perhaps puzzling to some is how privatized firms could grow so fast without improvement in access to bank finance, as indicated by the negative effect of privatization on the leverage ratio as well as the capital stock. The literature on finance in post-reform China has offered some clues. First, privatized firms resemble both de nova private firms and SOEs in some ways. Using the same data, Harrison et al. (2019) show that privatized firms have more access to finance than pure private firms, but less than SOEs. Similarly, Cull et al. (2015) find that firms with political connections, which include privatized firms, have fewer financial constraints than private firms. Privatized SOEs thus have better access to bank finance relative to their competitors. Second, substituting mechanisms may have provided alternative sources of financing. The foremost might be internal source of finance. Guariglia, Liu and Song (2011) provide firm-level evidence that high productivity and abundant cash flow explain why Chinese private firms were able to grow at a very fast rate. Allen, Qian and Qian (2005) provide evidence that reputation-based networks helped private firms grow in China. Cull, Xu and Zhu (2009) show that there was a spillover from bank credit by SOEs to private firms via trade credit, and as predicted by the Coase theorem (Coase, 1960), the spillover of bank credit from SOEs were especially strong when they have lower profitability and less investment opportunities. Finally, our results in this paper are suggestive that non-input and non-finance factors are also a critical part of the growth of privatized firms, be it labor/management efforts and quality.

section_name: Competition, central oversight, and the effects of privatization
text: We now examine how the effects of privatizations differ by market competition as well as non-economic objectives of privatized firms. We expect the effects of privatization to differ by the market power of the privatized firm. When an SOE has market power, after privatization it can increase profitability by restricting output or by sacrificing certain non-profit objectives (such as the provision of universal telephone services in the case of being a telecom operator). With substantial market power, privatization without a simultaneous introduction of competition will simply create private monopolies. Many economists therefore argue that privatization works best where there is competition limiting the market power of the incumbent(s) (Yarrow, 1986; Kay and Thompson, 1986; Vickers and Yarrow, 1998; Li and Xu, 2004). Competition is thus seen as a complement to privatization.

section_name: Competition, central oversight, and the effects of privatization
text: Similarly, the effects of privatization likely would be greater when privatized firms do not have stronger non-economic objectives. In this SOE restructuring program that features “grab the big and let go the small,” large SOEs, typically under central government oversight, often have strong control benefits to the government, and have non-economic objectives. They are the “commanding heights” industries that the (Socialist) government wants to retain strategic control, in the words of Vladimir Lenin (Yergin and Stanislaw, 1998; Bardhan, 2016; Huang et al., 2017). With other non-economic objectives in mind, the privatized firms may pursue efficiency half-heartedly—after all, pursuing non-economic objectives take economic resources and dampen economic incentives, hindering efficient resource allocation. We thus expect the privatization effects to be less positive for firms in “commanding heights” industries.

section_name: Competition, central oversight, and the effects of privatization
text: We proxy competition by industrial Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI). Similar to the practice in Table 5, we use the propensity-matched sample and incorporate the interaction of the privatization indicator Privatized with the industry-level HHI. The results are shown in Panel A of Table 7. We find that the effect of privatization is more pronounced among firms in industries with lower HHI, i.e., firms in more competitive industries. Specifically, for industries that are at the 75% percentile of HHI, privatization can boost firm TFP by 8.5%. In contrast, for industries that are at the 25% percentile of HHI, privatization can boost firm TFP by 14.9%. This result echoes the findings of Li and Xu (2004) that competition increased the gains from privatization for the telecommunications sector around the world.

section_name: Competition, central oversight, and the effects of privatization
text: We proxy “commanding heights” SOEs by the dummy variable of Central Oversight, which is one when the pre-privatized SOE was under the oversight of central government. Huang et al. (2017) show that SOEs under the oversight of the central government tend to be “commanding heights” SOEs, and the government wanted to retain stronger control over them. In Panel B of Table 7, we allow the privatization effects to differ by Central Oversight. Consistent with our expectation, the effects of privatization tend to be less positive for formerly central-oversight SOEs. The interaction term of the privatization dummy and Central Oversight is significantly negative for both TFP measures. Indeed, the privatization effect for central-oversight SOE is significantly negative.

section_name: Robustness checks
text: A concern is that our results could be sensitive to the way we measure productivity. We thus examine the robustness of our findings by employing three alternative measures of the firm’s productivity: the first measure (TFP_IN) uses the index number method to obtain TFP, which does not require estimating any parameters and directly use the estimates of the factor shares at the two-digit industry level from Saint-Paul and Bentolila (2003). The second measure (TFP_CS) also uses the index number method, but use cost shares from ASIF to capture the underlying technologies as in Hau, Huang and Wang (2019). See Appendix B for details of the constructions of these TFP measures. The third measure is the labor productivity (Labor Prod), calculated as ln (sales/employment). We use each of these proxies as the dependent variable, and report the simple OLS regression results with and without firm fixed effects, and the results with the propensity-matched sample, respectively. The results are illustrated in Table 8. Again, given the PSM results are most plausible, we again find that privatizations result in a gain in TFP of about 11-13 percentage points. The gain in labor productivity is about 7 percentage points. The evidence thus strongly corroborates our main findings.

section_name: Robustness checks
text: Furthermore, we conduct a series of robustness checks with varying matching strategies to strengthen the confidence on our findings. The results are reported in Table 9. First, we derive the matched control group based on a nearest neighbor matching approach, i.e., using the untreated observations with propensity score closest to the treated ones. The results are reported in Panel A of Table 9. Second, we use our baseline matching procedures but include additional control variables, i.e., the total sales to total assets (SalesA), the intangible assets to total assets (IntangA), the logarithm of total assets (Size), firm age (Age), the innovation captured by R&D Intensity (i.e., the ratio of R&D expenses to total sales), the ratio of new product sales to total sales (NewProd), the log value added (ln(VA)) and capital (Ln(K)). The results are reported in Panel B. Lastly, instead of using propensity score matching, we employ a “size and industry priority” criterion, i.e., for treated observation, we match it with an untreated one in the same industry with the closest firm size. The results are reported in Panel C. As shown in the table, all the results remain strong.

section_name: Conclusion
text: Our paper deals with two issues in the literature of privatizations: first, the lack of an overall assessment of the effects of privatization on productivity in China, the largest of its kind in the world, based on a widely-accepted and credible empirical framework, and second, the lack of understanding of how the effects of privatizing SOEs depend on industry competition and the importance of non-economic motives. Empirically, we rely on the matching-DID framework. We find that privatizations in China are robustly associated with productivity improvement within the range of effects found in the earlier literature on Eastern European and CIS countries, stably on the high end of the spectrum of those estimates at about 10 to 15 percent. There is suggestive evidence that the productivity effects of privatizations came from improvements in management effort and quality, and is more pronounced in more competitive industries. Privatizations did not improve productivity for “commanding-heights” SOEs that were under the central government’s oversight. Privatizations thus drastically transform the loss-making SOE sectors.

section_name: Conclusion
text: Given the robustly impressive effects of privatization in China, its privatization program should be viewed as a critical ingredient behind China’s growth since the mid-1990s. Recently, in many countries and perhaps especially in China, state capitalism where the government is strongly involved in running corporations has become more popular (Megginson 2017; Shirley and Xu, forthcoming). Indeed, in the past decade, China has reversed many of its previous marketization reforms include privatizations: some privatized firms were re-nationalized (Huang et al., 2021); the share of lending by state-owned banks to SOEs jumped from 28% in 2011 to 83% in 2016 (Lardy, 2019, p. 40), private firms without political connections became increasingly pessimistic on property rights protection and were reluctant to invest (Miao et al., 2024). The re-emergence of the state’s policies in favor of SOEs and against private firms, not surprisingly, has led to a pattern of loss-making behavior of SOEs that is utterly intuitive: the state’s protection of SOEs led to their inefficiency, and indeed, during the privatization period, the share of loss-making SOEs drastically drop from around 30% in 1998 to around 10 percent in 2010, and after the state began their “the state advances and the private retreats” policies, that share rose steadily and monotonously to 30% (Economist, August 10, 2024, p. 49-50). In other words, the pattern of state protection leading to wide-spread loss-making of SOEs has occurred twice—in the mid-1990s and now. In light of SOEs’ non-trivial share of the Chinese economy now (Huang et al. 2017), their rising share of loss-making, and China’s drastic slowing down in growth, reevaluating the role of privatizations in China’s growth has thus become critical, and further privatizations should be a top policy priority for China’s policy makers.
